Caption: Q-Q plot for Batch 2 'Viscosity at shear rate 10 1/s Here, Q-Q plots were employed to compare each batch against the theoretical quantiles of a lognormal distribution. Q-Q plots for batch 1 and batch 2 are illustrated in Fig 17 and 18, respectively. Both batches visually adhered to a lognormal distribution pattern, as indicated by the general alignment of data points along the reference line. To statistically assess the differences between these distributions, the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test was performed. The KS test is a robust non-parametric test that compares the cumulative distribution functions (CDFs) of two samples to determine if they originate from the same distribution. This test is particularly useful for its sensitivity to differences in both location and shape of the empirical CDFs of the sample data. The KS test statistic, denoted as D, is defined as the maximum absolute difference between the CDFs of the two samples. This statistic provides a quantitative measure of the divergence between the distributions, with a higher value of D indicating greater divergence. In our analysis, the KS test was applied to assess the conformity of the batch distributions with the theoretical lognormal distribution. The resulting D values were compared against critical values from the KS distribution to determine the statistical significance of the observed differences. A significant result would imply that the batches do not follow the same lognormal distribution, thus highlighting any potential discrepancies in the batch processes or underlying data generation mechanisms. By leveraging the KS test, we gain a deeper understanding of the statistical properties of the batches, ensuring a rigorous comparison that goes beyond visual inspection alone. This method provides a clear, quantitative basis for evaluating the similarity of the batch distributions, ultimately contributing to more reliable and robust conclusions in our analysis.
